text.title: A/B Test Design: Priority, Isolation, Sample Size, Significance
text.summary: Rules for designing valid ad A/B tests: testing priority order, single-variable isolation, minimum sample sizes, and significance thresholds.

section.title: Testing Priority

rule.statement: Test elements in this order: offer first, then hook/headline, then creative type, then visual style, then copy length, then CTA last.
rule.rationale: Higher-priority elements have larger effect sizes. Offer changes can produce 2-5x differences; CTA wording changes produce 5-15% differences. Testing CTA before offer wastes budget on low-leverage experiments when the offer may be the fundamental problem.

section.p: Frequency by element: Offer → monthly; Hook/headline → bi-weekly; Creative type → monthly; Visual style → monthly; Copy length → as needed; CTA → quarterly.

rule.statement: Change exactly one element per test; hold audience, budget, placement, optimization goal, and all other creative elements constant.
rule.rationale: If you change hook AND visual simultaneously and the variant wins, you cannot attribute the improvement to either element. You have wasted the test — you know the combined package is better, but you cannot apply the learning to future tests because you do not know which element drove it.

antipattern.description: Running a "fresh creative" test where headline, visual, copy, and format are all different between control and variant.
antipattern.reason: This is not a test — it is a coin flip between two complete creative packages. Neither outcome produces actionable learning about which element to iterate on.

section.title: Sample Size Requirements

rule.statement: Require 1,000+ impressions per variant for CTR tests; require 100+ conversions per variant for CPA tests; calculate minimum detectable effect before launching.
rule.rationale: Below these thresholds, variance dominates signal. A variant with 1.5% CTR from 300 impressions has a 95% confidence interval spanning 0.6%-2.4% — the result is meaningless. Pre-computing sample size sets the test duration and prevents premature stopping.

section.p: Minimum confidence thresholds: 95% for decisions that change account structure or kill a creative; 90% for directional learning; 80% for quick iteration when speed matters and the cost of a wrong call is low.

example.note: Quick significance check before declaring a winner.
example.code:
CTR: 1.0% (control) vs 1.2% (variant), +20% lift → Needs more data — could be noise at typical sample sizes.
CTR: 1.0% (control) vs 1.5% (variant), +50% lift → Likely significant with 1,000+ impressions each.
CTR: 1.0% (control) vs 2.0% (variant), +100% lift → Almost certainly significant; confirm with calculator.

section.title: Test Structure
section.p: Standard structure for manual tests: one campaign, one ad set (same audience for both), control ad and variant ad within that ad set. Use Meta's Experiments tool when budget allows ($200+ per variant) — it guarantees equal traffic split; default ad-set rotation does not.

rule.statement: Pre-register every test: document hypothesis, sample size, primary metric, decision rule, and stop date before launching. No retroactive metric switching.
rule.rationale: Retroactive metric switching (looking at 10 metrics and declaring a winner on whichever looks best) is p-hacking. Pre-registration creates accountability and prevents motivated reasoning when results are disappointing.
